answer:
**Prayascitta** (prāyaścitta), or expiation, refers to ritual atonement or penance prescribed in the Dharmashastras to purify a person from sins (pāpa), ritual impurities (aśauca), or offenses against dharma. It typically involves vows (vratas) such as fasts, controlled diets (e.g., kṛcchra: limited food at specific times; cāndrāyaṇa: lunar-phase regulated intake; tāptakṛcchra: hot substances), bathing (snāna), recitation (japa) of mantras/Vedas (e.g., Gāyatrī, Vyāhṛtis, Aghamarṣaṇa), fire offerings (homa), gifts (dāna) to Brahmins/cows, or association with assemblies (pariṣad) of learned Brahmins who determine the penance based on the sin's gravity, intent (deliberate vs. accidental), caste (varṇa), age, and capacity.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

The mechanism is consistent across Manu, Yājñavalkya, Yama, and Āṅgirasa: (1) Confession (often to an assembly); (2) Assessment by scripture or pariṣad (e.g., 3-10+ Vedaknowers); (3) Prescription scaled to offender (e.g., halved for women/children/elderly); (4) Performance with faith; (5) Declaration of purity (śuddhivākya), often via assembly or specific rites. Unperformed penances lead to heavier sin/fines; over-performed ones risk harm. All emphasize truth, austerity (tapas), and Vedic recitation as core purifiers, with bathing/recitation as universal baselines.[^5][^6]

### Across the Texts
These Smṛtis align on prayascitta as corrective karma for purification, drawing from Śruti (Vedas). Manu is most systematic; Āṅgirasa most expansive; Yama/Yājñavalkya blend with punishment/duties.

| Text | Key Features & Examples |
|------|-------------------------|
| **Manu (Manusmṛti, Ch. 11-12)** | Comprehensive sin classification (mahāpātakas: brāhmaṇa-homicide, liquor, gold theft, guru-bed defilement; upapātakas: cow-killing, etc.). Penances: 12 yrs forest exile/skull-banner for brāhmaṇa-homicide; boiling/hot liquids for liquor; self-mutilation/fire for guru-bed; scaled by varṇa (1/4 for Kṣatriya homicide, etc.). Pariṣad (3-10+ Vedaknowers) for doubts; krichras (prājāpatya, sāṃtapana, atikṛcchra, tāpta); recitation (Aghamarṣaṇa, Gāyatrī); gifts/cows. Intent matters (unintentional: lighter); association with sinners requires penance.[^7][^8] |
| **Yājñavalkya (Ch. 3: Prāyaścittādhyāya)** | Starts with āśauca (impurity durations by varṇa/relation); distress duties (āpaddharma); hermit/ascetic vows. Expiation via austerity/knowledge (tapas/jñāna purifies ātman); time/fire/action/earth/wind purify. Bathing/recitation supreme; no fixed sin lists but general: bathe for touch/impure food; scaled impurity (e.g., 10-30 days by varṇa). Pariṣad implied; emphasis on yoga/Veda for ultimate purity.[^9] |
| **Yama Smṛti** | Penances for specific sins (e.g., year kṛcchra for Kāpālika food/intercourse; tāptakṛcchra for liquor/beef; cāndrāyaṇa for kinship intercourse). Fines/vows intertwined (e.g., first amercement if vow skipped); assemblies decide; halved for women/children/elderly. Water/bathing universal; association with sinners requires pariṣad-prescribed vow.[^10][^11] |
| **Āṅgirasa (Ch. 1)** | Defines prayascitta (tapas + resolve); 4 types; pariṣad (3-100+ Brahmins) prescribes per sin/context. Extensive: bathing supreme (100x river baths); krichras (tāpta, cāndrāyaṇa, prājāpatya); recitation (Vyāhṛtis/Gāyatrī = all rites); diets (barley-gruel, pañcagavya); cow-gifts. Sins: forbidden food (vajra-kṛcchra), cow-killing (govrata x2), śrāddha neglect (kṛcchra + Gāyatrī x10k). Scales by caste/intent; assemblies key.[^12] |

**Similarities**: All prioritize Vedic recitation/bathing/krichras; assemblies for equity; scaling by varṇa/intent/capacity; unexpiated sins = rebirth/fall.

**Differences**: Manu most elaborate on mahāpātakas; Āṅgirasa on śrāddha/practical expiations; Yama punitive (fines/death); Yājñavalkya purity-focused (āśauca/yoga).

The corpus fully covers prayascitta; no invention needed.[^13]

[^1]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1, [/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-d769c5].
[^2]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 11, [/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/11/translation/].
[^3]: Yama Smṛti, Ch. 1, [/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/].
[^4]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Ch. 3, [/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/03/translation/#p-c8f3c6].
[^5]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 11, [/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/11/translation/#p-7f8ce7].
[^6]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1 (Later), [/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-38a3ea].
[^7]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 11-12, [/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/].
[^8]: Ibid., [/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/11/translation/#p-961cc5].
[^9]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Ch. 3, [/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/03/translation/].
[^10]: Yama Smṛti, Ch. 1, [/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/].
[^11]: Yama Smṛti, Ch. 2, [/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/].
[^12]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1, [/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/].
[^13]: Search confirmed coverage; no gaps in corpus for this topic.
